stem: Use Green's theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \left( [[p]]xy\,dx + [[q]]x^{2}\,dy \right) \), where \( C \) is the boundary of the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \), oriented counterclockwise.
solution: With \( P=[[p]]xy \) and \( Q=[[q]]x^{2} \), Green's theorem gives \( \oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy = \iint_D (\partial_x Q - \partial_y P)\,dA = \iint_D \big(2([[q]])x - ([[p]])x\big)\,dA \) over \( [0,[[W]]]\times[0,[[H]]] \), which equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the orientation, giving the negative of the correct circulation.
This forgets to also subtract off the \( \partial_y P \) contribution correctly and integrates only the \( Q_x \) piece with a sign slip.
This adds \( \partial_x Q + \partial_y P \) instead of subtracting, using the wrong Green's-theorem integrand.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use Green's theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \left( [[p]]y^{2}\,dx + [[q]]x\,dy \right) \), where \( C \) is the boundary of the triangular region \( D \) described (as a Type II region) by \( 0 \le y \le [[H]],\; 0 \le x \le [[m]]y \) (a triangle with a vertex at the origin), oriented counterclockwise.
solution: With \( P=[[p]]y^{2} \) and \( Q=[[q]]x \), Green's theorem gives \( \oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy = \iint_D (\partial_x Q - \partial_y P)\,dA = \iint_D \big([[q]] - 2([[p]])y\big)\,dA \). Describing the triangular region as a Type II region (\(0\le y\le [[H]]\), \(0\le x\le [[m]]y\)) and integrating \( x \) first gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the orientation, giving the negative of the correct circulation.
This treats the integrand as if it were the CONSTANT value at \( y=[[H]] \) times the triangle's area, instead of integrating the \( y \)-dependence.
This flips the sign of the \( \partial_y P \) contribution before multiplying by the triangle's area.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use Green's theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \left( [[p]]y\,dx + [[q]]x\,dy \right) \), where \( C \) is the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[R]]^{2} \), oriented clockwise.
solution: With \( P=[[p]]y \) and \( Q=[[q]]x \), Green's theorem for the STANDARD counterclockwise orientation gives \( \iint_D (\partial_x Q-\partial_y P)\,dA=\big(([[q]])-([[p]])\big)\pi([[R]])^{2} \). Since \( C \) here is traversed CLOCKWISE (the opposite orientation), the integral is the negative of that: \( -\big(([[q]])-([[p]])\big)\pi([[R]])^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to flip the sign for the clockwise orientation, reporting the counterclockwise value instead.
This adds \( \partial_x Q + \partial_y P \) instead of subtracting, using the wrong Green's-theorem integrand.
This double counts, as if traversing the circle twice.
This halves the correct circulation.
This adds a spurious \( \pi \) to the correct value.
This subtracts a spurious \( \pi \) from the correct value.

stem: Use the flux (normal) form of Green's theorem, \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n}\,ds = \iint_D \left(\partial_x P + \partial_y Q\right)dA \), to evaluate the outward flux of \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = \left( [[p]]x^{2},\; [[q]]y^{2} \right) \) across \( C \), the boundary of the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \).
solution: The divergence form of Green's theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n}\,ds = \iint_D (\partial_x P + \partial_y Q)\,dA \). Here \( \partial_x P + \partial_y Q = 2([[p]])x + 2([[q]])y \); integrating over the rectangle gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the sign of the flux reversed.
This treats \( \partial_x P + \partial_y Q \) as the CONSTANT \( [[p]]+[[q]] \), forgetting the integrand varies linearly with \( x,y \).
This uses \( \partial_x P - \partial_y Q \) (the circulation curl) instead of the divergence needed for the flux form.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
